title: Group Decision-Making
summary: How teams generate options and commit to a choice, tuned to PMBOK 8: decision rules, Delphi, weighted matrices and the quick consensus tools agile teams use. Decision-making sits inside the Stakeholders and Resources performance domains and the Build an Empowered Culture and Be an Accountable Leader principles - collaborate to find the option that delivers the most value.
intro: Pick the right method for the call: consensus, majority, Delphi, fist-of-five and more.

sections.heading: Why this matters
sections.body: PMBOK 8 is principle- and outcome-based. Group decision-making lives in the Stakeholders and Resources performance domains and reflects the Build an Empowered Culture and Be an Accountable Leader principles.

blocks.title: Frame every choice
lines: Collaborate first - decisions are made with the team, not to the team.
Aim for the option that delivers the most value, not just the fastest yes.
Leadership is a behaviour: facilitate, don't dictate.
Match the effort to the stakes - lightweight for routine calls, structured for high-impact ones.

sections.heading: How groups decide
sections.body: The decision rule sets the bar for agreement. Agree it before the discussion starts, so everyone accepts the outcome it produces.

groups.label: Bar
lines: Everyone agrees.

groups.label: Trade-off
lines: Strongest buy-in, but slow.
Delphi and fist-of-five help reach it.

groups.label: Bar
lines: More than half agree (over 50%).

groups.label: Trade-off
lines: Common and quick.

groups.label: Bar
lines: Largest single block wins, even if under half.

groups.label: Trade-off
lines: Used when the vote splits and no one has a majority.

groups.label: Bar
lines: One person decides for the group.

groups.label: Trade-off
lines: Fastest, but the weakest buy-in.

blocks.title: Consensus is the agile aim
blocks.text: Most agile teams aim for consensus - broad agreement everyone can live with and support - rather than strict unanimity.

blocks.title: Don't confuse these
lines: Plurality = the biggest group wins (no majority needed).
Majority = over half.
Consent = no reasoned objection (not the same as full agreement).

sections.heading: Which technique, and when
sections.body: Match the technique to the decision. This is the fastest way to answer 'the team needs to decide - what should they use?' questions.

rows.value: High-stakes call that needs total buy-in; you can afford the time.
Routine or time-boxed call; over half is enough to commit.
Several options split the vote and no one reaches a majority.
Want fast alignment - proceed unless someone raises a reasoned, paramount objection.
Emergency or trivial call the accountable person can simply make.
Expert judgement under uncertainty; anonymity needed to kill bias.
Long list to narrow - each person spends a fixed set of votes to surface the top few.
Quick, visible read of consensus in the room before committing.

blocks.title: Cue to spot
lines: 'Anonymous + experts + rounds' -> Delphi.
'Narrow a big list of options' -> multi-voting / dot voting.
'Gauge agreement fast' -> fist-of-five or Roman voting.
'Score against cost, risk, value' -> weighted decision matrix (MCDA).

sections.heading: Generating options
sections.body: Good decisions start with a wide set of ideas, generated before any are judged.

items.title: Brainstorming
lines: Produce many ideas freely.
Defer all criticism until later.

items.title: Nominal group technique
lines: Brainstorm, then privately rank the ideas.
Louder voices don't dominate the outcome.

items.title: Mind mapping
lines: Branch related ideas from a central theme.
Reveals structure and links.

items.title: Affinity diagram
lines: Cluster a large pile of ideas into natural groupings.
Makes sense of the mess.

blocks.title: Remember
blocks.text: Nominal group technique = brainstorming PLUS private voting, so the loudest person in the room doesn't win.

sections.heading: Delphi and weighted decision matrices
sections.body: Two structured techniques remove bias from harder judgements.

groups.label: How
lines: A panel of experts responds in anonymous rounds.
A facilitator summarises each round and feeds it back until answers converge.

groups.label: Why
lines: Anonymity removes seniority bias, bandwagon effects and groupthink.

groups.label: Use for
lines: Estimates, risk and forecasts.

groups.label: How
lines: Score each option against weighted criteria such as cost, risk and value.
Total the weighted scores.

groups.label: Why
lines: Compares options objectively rather than on gut feel.

groups.label: Also called
lines: Multi-criteria decision analysis (MCDA).

blocks.title: The point of Delphi
blocks.text: Delphi = anonymous + iterative + expert. The anonymity is the whole point - it kills bias and bandwagons.

sections.heading: Quick agile consensus tools
sections.body: Agile teams use fast, visible techniques to gauge agreement.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 100' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='30' y1='48' x2='300' y2='48' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.3'/><circle cx='30' cy='48' r='6' fill='#B5462F'/><circle cx='84' cy='48' r='6' fill='#B5462F' fill-opacity='0.55'/><circle cx='138' cy='48' r='6' fill='#C98A1F'/><circle cx='192' cy='48' r='6' fill='#C98A1F' fill-opacity='0.7'/><circle cx='246' cy='48' r='6' fill='#2F6F5E' fill-opacity='0.7'/><circle cx='300' cy='48' r='6' fill='#2F6F5E'/><text x='30' y='32' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>0</text><text x='84' y='32' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>1</text><text x='138' y='32' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>2</text><text x='192' y='32' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>3</text><text x='246' y='32' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>4</text><text x='300' y='32' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>5</text><text x='30' y='72' fill='#B5462F' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>block</text><text x='300' y='72' fill='#2F6F5E' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>full support</text><text x='165' y='92' fill='currentColor' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>below 3 = keep discussing before you proceed</text></svg>
figure.caption: Fist of five: 0 blocks, 3 is 'can live with it', 5 is full support.

items.title: Fist of five (fist to five)
lines: On a count, everyone shows 0 to 5 fingers.
Fist (0) = block or strong objection; 5 = enthusiastic support; 3 = 'I can live with it'.
Anything below 3 is discussed before proceeding.

items.title: Roman voting
lines: Thumbs up, sideways or down.
Instant read of agreement.

items.title: Dot voting
lines: Each person places a fixed number of dots on options they favour.
A form of multi-voting to narrow a list.

items.title: Planning poker
lines: Estimators reveal cards at the same moment.
No one anchors on the first number spoken.

rows.value: Block / strong objection - stop and discuss.
I can live with it - acceptable.

blocks.title: Threshold rule
blocks.text: A 1 or 2 means 'I have concerns' - keep discussing. You don't proceed until everyone is at 3 or above.

sections.heading: Matching approach to decision
sections.body: Whatever rule you use, agree it before the discussion starts, so everyone accepts the outcome it produces.

rows.value: Aim for consensus or unanimity, accepting it is slower.
Majority, plurality, or let the accountable person decide.

blocks.title: Golden rule
blocks.text: Agree the decision rule up front. Changing the rule after you see the result destroys trust.

source: PM Cram — aligned to the current PMBOK Guide and PMI Examination Content Outline.
